concepts.name: Five-Phase Business Model Design Process
concepts.definition: A structured, iterative framework for business model innovation: (1) Mobilize — prepare for a successful project; (2) Understand — research and analyze the elements needed for the effort; (3) Design — generate and test viable options, select the best; (4) Implement — execute the model in the real world; (5) Manage — adapt and adjust the model in response to market evolution.

concepts.name: Mobilize
concepts.definition: Phase 1: Assemble all the elements for a successful business model design project. Activities: frame project objectives, test initial ideas, plan, assemble team. Key success factors: right people, experience, and knowledge. Danger: overestimating the value of the initial idea.

concepts.name: Understand
concepts.definition: Phase 2: Research and analyze the elements needed for the business model design effort. Activities: scan environment, study potential customers, interview experts, study previous attempts, collect ideas and opinions. Key success factor: deep understanding beyond superficial data. Danger: over-researching and never starting to design.

concepts.name: Design
concepts.definition: Phase 3: Adapt and modify the business model in response to an ongoing market evolution. Activities: generate many model concepts (brainstorming), prototype, test, select. Key success factor: avoiding premature commitment to one idea. Danger: watering down bold ideas.

concepts.name: Implement
concepts.definition: Phase 4: Execute the business model design in the real world. Activities: communicate and involve, execute. Key success factor: generating buy-in from the organization. Danger: letting momentum fade.

concepts.name: Manage
concepts.definition: Phase 5: Set up the management structures to continuously monitor, evaluate, and adapt or transform the business model. Activities: scan environment, assess business model continuously, rejuvenate or rethink. Key success factor: long-term perspective. Danger: becoming a victim of your own success.

arguments.claim: Business model innovation is a continuous cycle, not a one-time project
arguments.evidence: The five-phase process is deliberately circular. After the Manage phase, new understanding from the market feeds back to new design iterations. Companies that treat the Canvas as a one-time exercise (fill it once, frame it) miss the point — it's a living map that should evolve as the business and market evolve.

arguments.claim: Each phase has a distinct primary danger, and knowing the danger is half the prevention
arguments.evidence: Mobilize: overvaluing the initial idea. Understand: analysis paralysis. Design: watering down bold ideas to make them 'acceptable.' Implement: momentum fading after the initial excitement. Manage: becoming a victim of your own success by not adapting.

logicChains.description: Mobilize (assemble team + frame objective) → Understand (research customers + environment + prior attempts) → Design (brainstorm → prototype → test → select) → Implement (communicate → execute → measure) → Manage (monitor → evaluate → adapt → back to Design when needed)
logicChains.sourceHint: Chapter 5 of the book lays this out as a distinct process framework with detailed activities per phase

nuance: The process is not linear in practice. You may go back to Understand from Design, or from Implement back to Design. The phases are mindsets, not rigid stage gates.
Good process doesn't guarantee good outcomes — but bad process (skipping customer understanding, not prototyping, committing to the first idea) almost guarantees mediocre outcomes.
